learning_objectives: Understand the fundamental network architectures used in multiplayer games
Compare client-server and peer-to-peer topologies with their trade-offs
Identify which architecture suits different game genres
readings: Multiplayer Game Programming Ch. 1-2 by Joshua Glazer and Sanjay Madhav
Gaffer on Games: What Every Programmer Needs to Know About Game Networking

lecture_notes.introduction: Multiplayer games are among the most technically demanding software projects in existence. This course covers the design and engineering challenges of building games that connect players across networks.
key_concepts: Client-server architecture: authoritative server, thin vs. thick clients
Peer-to-peer architecture: mesh, star, and hybrid topologies
Dedicated servers vs. listen servers vs. relay servers
Bandwidth, latency, and packet loss as fundamental constraints
The CAP theorem applied to game state: consistency, availability, partition tolerance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Network topology visualization showing packet flow in client-server vs. P2P for a 4-player game
setup_requirements: Unity 2023.2+ with Netcode for GameObjects installed
Local network test environment (two machines or virtual network)

review.value: Server is authoritative over game state; prevents most cheating; scales with server hardware; single point of failure
No dedicated server cost; lower latency between nearby peers; difficult to prevent cheating; scales poorly beyond 8-16 players

learning_objectives: Implement reliable and unreliable state synchronization methods
Understand the difference between state transfer and input transfer models
Apply snapshot interpolation and delta compression techniques
readings: Valve Developer Community: Source Multiplayer Networking

lecture_notes.introduction: The core challenge of multiplayer game development is keeping game state synchronized across multiple machines connected by an unreliable, high-latency network. Every design decision in this course flows from this fundamental problem.
key_concepts: State synchronization vs. input synchronization (lockstep)
Snapshot interpolation: buffering server snapshots and interpolating between them
Delta compression: sending only what changed since the last acknowledged snapshot
Reliable vs. unreliable delivery: when to use each and why
Object relevancy and interest management: don't send what the client doesn't need
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing basic snapshot interpolation in Unity Netcode, visualizing the interpolation buffer
setup_requirements: Unity Netcode for GameObjects project from session 1
Network simulation tools for adding artificial latency

review.value: Client renders state from the past (typically 100ms behind server), interpolating between two buffered snapshots for smooth visuals
Instead of sending full game state each tick, only transmit fields that changed since the client's last acknowledged state, reducing bandwidth by 80-95%

learning_objectives: Implement client-side prediction with server reconciliation
Understand and implement lag compensation for hit detection
Analyze the trade-offs between responsiveness and fairness
readings: Overwatch GDC Talk: Netcode and Latency Compensation

lecture_notes.introduction: Players expect instant feedback from their inputs, but network latency means the server cannot confirm actions for tens or hundreds of milliseconds. Latency compensation techniques bridge this gap to create the illusion of responsiveness.
key_concepts: Client-side prediction: simulate locally, reconcile with server authority
Server reconciliation: when the server corrects your prediction, replay unacknowledged inputs
Lag compensation for hit detection: rewinding server state to the shooter's perspective
The peekers advantage problem and how different games handle it
Jitter buffers and adaptive interpolation delay
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a client-side predicted character controller with server reconciliation and misprediction correction
setup_requirements: Unity Netcode project with basic movement synchronized
Network simulation tool configured for 50ms, 100ms, and 200ms round-trip latency

review.value: Client simulates input locally for instant feedback, stores input history, and replays from the last server-confirmed state when corrections arrive
Server rewinds world state to the time the shooting client perceived when they fired, then performs hit detection against that historical state

learning_objectives: Design matchmaking systems that produce fair and enjoyable matches
Understand Elo, Glicko-2, and TrueSkill rating systems
Implement queue management and match quality optimization
readings: Riot Games Engineering: Under the Hood of League of Legends Matchmaking

lecture_notes.introduction: Matchmaking determines who plays with and against whom. A great matchmaking system makes every match feel competitive and fair. A poor one drives players away faster than any bug.
key_concepts: Rating systems: Elo (chess-derived, 1v1), Glicko-2 (adds confidence intervals), TrueSkill (supports teams)
Match quality metrics: skill gap, queue time, ping, party size balance
Queue management: balancing wait time against match quality
Placement matches and confidence intervals for new players
Smurf detection and rank manipulation prevention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating 1000 matches with TrueSkill ratings and analyzing convergence speed and match quality distribution
setup_requirements: Python environment with numpy for rating simulations
Spreadsheet for analyzing match quality distributions

review.value: Models each player as a Gaussian distribution (mu = skill estimate, sigma = uncertainty). Updates both after each match. Supports team-based games natively.
As queue time increases, progressively widen acceptable skill range. Typical target: 95% of matches start within 2 minutes with skill gap under 1 standard deviation.

learning_objectives: Identify common cheating vectors in multiplayer games
Design server-authoritative systems that prevent client-side manipulation
Understand the role and limitations of anti-cheat middleware
readings: Game Security: Naughty Dog GDC Talk on The Last of Us Multiplayer

lecture_notes.introduction: Cheating destroys competitive integrity and drives honest players away. Anti-cheat is not a single system but a design philosophy that must be embedded into every layer of your multiplayer architecture.
key_concepts: Client-side cheats: wallhacks, aimbots, speed hacks, memory manipulation
Server authority as the primary defense: never trust the client
Server-side validation: movement speed checks, fire rate limits, impossible action detection
Statistical detection: identifying aim patterns, reaction times, and win rates that deviate from human norms
Anti-cheat middleware: kernel-level (EasyAntiCheat, Vanguard) vs. user-level (BattlEye) and privacy trade-offs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing server-side movement validation that detects speed hacks and teleportation while tolerating legitimate network jitter
setup_requirements: Networked Unity project with client-server movement
Cheat simulation scripts for testing detection
Logging framework for recording suspicious actions

review.value: The server must validate every client action against game rules. The client is a rendering terminal, not a trusted participant in game logic.
Track per-player metrics (headshot %, reaction time distribution, movement patterns) and flag statistical outliers for review. Avoids false positives from single-event detection.

learning_objectives: Design server infrastructure that scales with player demand
Plan live operations including content updates, events, and maintenance windows
Implement monitoring, alerting, and incident response procedures
readings: AWS Game Tech: Scaling Game Servers with Amazon GameLift
Bungie GDC Talk: Destiny 2 Live Service Operations

lecture_notes.introduction: Shipping a multiplayer game is the beginning, not the end. Live operations keep the game healthy, the servers running, and the players engaged over months and years.
key_concepts: Server infrastructure: dedicated vs. cloud (AWS GameLift, Azure PlayFab, GCP Agones)
Auto-scaling: scaling server fleet based on player count, time of day, and geographic demand
Zero-downtime deployments: rolling updates, blue-green deployment, feature flags
Monitoring and alerting: server health, player count, error rates, latency percentiles
Incident response: runbooks, on-call rotations, communication to players during outages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a basic auto-scaling game server fleet on AWS GameLift with CloudWatch monitoring and alerts
setup_requirements: AWS free tier account or simulated environment
Docker for containerized game server builds

review.value: Scale based on active match count vs. available capacity. Target 70-80% utilization. Pre-scale for known events (launches, tournaments, weekends).
Run new version alongside old version. Route new matches to new servers. Let existing matches finish on old servers. Drain and terminate old fleet.

questions.question: A multiplayer FPS game uses client-side prediction with server reconciliation. The client sends input at tick 100 and immediately simulates the result locally. The server processes tick 100 and sends back a correction at tick 105 (50ms later). Describe step-by-step what happens on the client when the correction arrives and the predicted position differs from the server position by 0.5 units.
questions.answer: When the correction arrives, the client: (1) Receives the authoritative server state for tick 100. (2) Compares it to the predicted state at tick 100 and detects a 0.5-unit discrepancy. (3) Snaps the authoritative state to tick 100 as the new base state. (4) Replays all unacknowledged inputs from ticks 101-105 on top of the corrected state. (5) The resulting position after replay is the new predicted position, which should be close to but slightly different from the original prediction. (6) Optionally smooths the visual correction over several frames to avoid visual popping.

rubric.key: Identifies server state as authoritative starting point
Describes input replay from corrected state
Mentions visual smoothing or snap correction
Correctly identifies which ticks need replay (101-105)

questions.question: Explain why delta compression is essential for a 64-player battle royale game and calculate the approximate bandwidth savings. Assume the full game state snapshot is 50KB and typically only 5% of fields change between ticks at 20 ticks per second.
questions.answer: Without delta compression: 50KB * 20 ticks/sec = 1000KB/sec = 1MB/sec per client. With 64 clients: 64MB/sec total server upload bandwidth. With delta compression (5% change rate): 50KB * 0.05 = 2.5KB per delta * 20 ticks/sec = 50KB/sec per client. With 64 clients: 3.2MB/sec total. Savings: 95% bandwidth reduction, from 64MB/sec to 3.2MB/sec. This makes the game viable on both server and client connections. Without delta compression, most home internet connections could not handle the downstream bandwidth.

rubric.key: Explains why this matters for feasibility

questions.question: Design a matchmaking system for a 5v5 team-based competitive game. Specify: (a) which rating system you would use and why, (b) how you handle parties of mixed skill levels queuing together, (c) your strategy for balancing queue time against match quality, and (d) how you handle placement matches for new players.
questions.answer: (a) TrueSkill 2 or Glicko-2 adapted for teams, because they model uncertainty (sigma) which is critical for new players and returning players. (b) For mixed-skill parties, use the highest-rated player's MMR weighted upward (e.g., party MMR = avg + 0.5 * (max - avg)) to prevent boosting. (c) Start with tight skill bounds (within 200 MMR) and widen by 50 MMR every 30 seconds of queue time, capping at 500 MMR difference. (d) New players start with high sigma (uncertainty). First 10 matches are placement matches with accelerated sigma updates. Match them against a mix of established players to quickly calibrate.

rubric.key: Chooses appropriate rating system with valid justification
Addresses mixed-skill party handling with anti-boosting measures
Describes progressive skill bound widening with specific numbers
Explains placement match system using uncertainty/sigma

questions.question: A player reports that an opponent in your competitive FPS game is using an aimbot. Describe three different layers of anti-cheat detection your game should have that could catch this cheat, and explain why relying on only one layer is insufficient.
questions.answer: Layer 1 - Server-side validation: The server tracks fire rate, checks that the client's reported aim direction is physically possible given previous aim position and human reaction time limits. Catches blatant aimbots that snap instantly. Layer 2 - Statistical analysis: Over many matches, track headshot percentage, time-to-target, aim flick patterns, and accuracy distribution. An aimbot produces inhuman consistency that deviates from natural variance. Catches subtle aimbots that add randomization. Layer 3 - Client-side anti-cheat (e.g., EasyAntiCheat): Scans for known cheat signatures in memory, detects code injection, and monitors for suspicious processes. Catches commercially available cheats. No single layer is sufficient because: server-side validation can only catch blatant cheats, statistical analysis requires many matches of data before flagging, and client-side anti-cheat can be bypassed by custom or new cheats. Defense in depth is essential.

rubric.key: Describes server-side validation with specific checks
Describes statistical detection with specific metrics
Explains why defense in depth is necessary

major_assignments.title: Networked Multiplayer Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a functional multiplayer game prototype supporting 2-4 players with authoritative server architecture, client-side prediction, and lag compensation. The prototype must demonstrate smooth gameplay under simulated latency conditions of up to 200ms round-trip.
objectives: Implement a client-server architecture with authoritative game state
Build client-side prediction with server reconciliation for player movement
Implement lag compensation for at least one game mechanic (e.g., hit detection)
Demonstrate the system working under various simulated network conditions
deliverables: Unity project with Netcode for GameObjects implementation
Server and client builds that can run on separate machines
Network simulation test results showing behavior at 0ms, 50ms, 100ms, and 200ms RTT
Technical documentation explaining the networking architecture and design decisions
Video demonstration showing gameplay under normal and degraded network conditions

criteria: Server is fully authoritative over game state
Client never directly modifies authoritative state
State synchronization is bandwidth-efficient
Connection handling is robust (join, leave, reconnect)
Client-side prediction produces smooth local movement
Server reconciliation correctly handles mispredictions
Lag compensation produces fair hit detection across latencies
Visual correction is smooth and not jarring to players
Gameplay remains playable at 200ms RTT
Handles packet loss gracefully without desynchronization
Server handles player disconnection without crashing
No exploitable client-side state manipulation
Clear architecture diagram showing data flow
Explanation of synchronization strategy and trade-offs
Network test results with analysis
Identified limitations and proposed improvements
Fully functional multiplayer prototype with authoritative server
Smooth gameplay demonstrated across latency conditions
Robust handling of network edge cases
Thorough documentation of architecture and design decisions

major_assignments.title: Competitive Multiplayer Systems Design Document
major_assignments.description: Design and document the complete competitive multiplayer systems for a team-based game. This design document must cover matchmaking, ranking, anti-cheat, social systems, and live operations. All systems must be specified in enough detail for an engineering team to implement them.
objectives: Design a complete matchmaking and ranking system with mathematical specification
Specify anti-cheat measures across server-side, statistical, and client-side layers
Design social systems that encourage positive player behavior
Plan live operations infrastructure including scaling, deployment, and monitoring
deliverables: Matchmaking specification: rating algorithm, queue logic, party handling, placement system
Anti-cheat specification: server-side validation rules, statistical detection thresholds, client-side integration plan
Social systems design: friend system, party system, reporting, reputation scoring
Infrastructure plan: server architecture diagram, auto-scaling rules, deployment pipeline, monitoring dashboard specification
Simulation results: matchmaking simulation with 10,000 simulated players showing rating convergence and match quality

criteria: Rating system is mathematically specified with update formulas
Queue logic handles edge cases (long queues, skill extremes, parties)
Placement system is well-defined with convergence analysis
Anti-smurf and anti-boosting measures are included
Server-side validation covers all major cheat categories
Statistical detection has defined thresholds and confidence intervals
False positive mitigation strategy is included
Escalation and ban appeal process is defined
Social systems are fully specified and integrated with matchmaking
Infrastructure plan supports the designed systems at scale
Monitoring covers all critical system health metrics
Deployment strategy supports zero-downtime updates
Simulation is methodologically sound with sufficient sample size
Results demonstrate rating convergence within expected matches
Match quality metrics are defined and analyzed
Edge cases are identified and addressed
Implementation-ready specification of all competitive systems
Mathematically rigorous matchmaking and rating design
Comprehensive anti-cheat strategy with defense in depth
Realistic infrastructure plan with simulation validation

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of networking concepts and multiplayer design knowledge

lab_exercises.description: Hands-on networking implementation exercises completed during lab sessions

multiplayer_prototype.description: Functional networked multiplayer prototype with latency compensation

systems_design_document.description: Comprehensive competitive multiplayer systems design with simulation analysis

final_presentation.description: Live demonstration and defense of multiplayer prototype and systems design

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions where networked testing requires multiple participants. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Network programming assignments require partner testing; late submissions impact your testing partners and will be penalized accordingly.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. The multiplayer prototype may be completed in teams of up to 3. The systems design document must be completed individually. All team contributions must be documented.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original or properly attributed. Using networking middleware is permitted and encouraged, but the integration and game logic must be your own. Code sharing between teams is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a computer capable of running Unity with networking packages. Two networked machines (physical or virtual) are required for testing. Lab computers are available for scheduled testing sessions.
